title: WHO Definition of Rare Diseases
text: A rare disease is generally defined as one affecting fewer than 1 in 2,000 people in the general population. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), there are between 6,000 and 8,000 known rare diseases, and approximately 300 million people worldwide are living with a rare disease. About 80% of rare diseases are genetic in origin, and many are life-threatening or chronically debilitating. Most rare diseases begin in childhood. WHO emphasizes that early diagnosis, access to treatment, and cross-border collaboration are critical pillars of rare disease management.

title: WHO Essential Medicines for Rare Diseases
text: The WHO Model List of Essential Medicines includes a growing number of medicines for rare diseases, including enzyme replacement therapies, substrate reduction therapies, and gene therapies. WHO advocates for equitable access to these medicines, particularly in low- and middle-income countries. Orphan drugs are developed under special regulatory frameworks that incentivize pharmaceutical companies to develop treatments for rare conditions.

title: WHO Genetic Testing and Counselling Guidelines
text: WHO recommends that genetic counselling be offered to individuals and families who may be at risk of an inherited condition. Genetic testing for rare diseases should be paired with pre-test and post-test counselling. Cascade screening of family members is recommended when an autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, or X-linked condition is identified. Whole exome sequencing (WES) and whole genome sequencing (WGS) are increasingly used for undiagnosed rare disease cases.

title: WHO Emergency Care — Recognizing Clinical Deterioration
text: WHO emergency care guidelines emphasize the ABCDE approach for all critically ill patients: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability (neurological), and Exposure. For patients with rare metabolic diseases, clinical deterioration may be triggered by infection, fasting, surgery, or physiological stress. Early recognition of metabolic crises, hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, and hyperammonemia is essential. Healthcare providers should be alerted that standard treatments may be contraindicated in certain rare disease patients.

title: WHO Non-communicable Diseases — Multimorbidity Management
text: Patients with rare diseases often carry a significant burden of multimorbidity. WHO recommends an integrated person-centred care approach, coordinating care across specialties, managing polypharmacy risks, and supporting mental health alongside physical health. Multidisciplinary teams (MDTs) are the gold standard for rare disease case management, typically including a specialist physician, clinical geneticist, physiotherapist, dietitian, and social worker.

title: NIH National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences — Rare Disease Definition and Resources
text: The NIH National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) defines a rare disease in the United States as one affecting fewer than 200,000 Americans. NIH's Undiagnosed Diseases Network (UDN) provides diagnostic evaluations for patients with undiagnosed or rare conditions. The NIH Rare Diseases Clinical Research Network (RDCRN) funds research consortia studying over 200 rare diseases. NIH's Genetic and Rare Diseases Information Center (GARD) maintains a publicly searchable database of rare diseases.

title: NIH Diagnostic Workup for Undiagnosed Rare Diseases
text: NIH recommends a systematic approach to diagnosing undiagnosed rare diseases: (1) thorough clinical history and physical examination, (2) standard biochemical and metabolic workup, (3) targeted gene panel testing based on clinical phenotype, (4) whole exome sequencing (WES) if panel is negative, (5) whole genome sequencing (WGS) for remaining cases, (6) RNA sequencing for variant interpretation. Functional studies may be required to confirm pathogenicity. Phenotype-driven approaches using tools like PhenoDB or Phenomizer can help match HPO terms to candidate diagnoses.

title: NIH Guidelines — Lysosomal Storage Disorders
text: Lysosomal storage disorders (LSDs) are a group of approximately 50 inherited metabolic diseases caused by deficiencies in lysosomal enzymes or transport proteins. Common LSDs include Gaucher disease (GBA gene), Fabry disease (GLA gene), Pompe disease (GAA gene), Niemann-Pick disease, and MPS disorders. Diagnosis is confirmed by enzyme activity assay and/or molecular genetic testing. Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) is available for several LSDs. Biomarkers such as lyso-Gb1 (Gaucher), lyso-Gb3 (Fabry), and Hex4 (Pompe) are used for monitoring. Newborn screening programs are expanding to include several LSDs.

title: NIH Guidelines — Mitochondrial Disease
text: Mitochondrial diseases are caused by mutations in either mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) or nuclear DNA genes encoding mitochondrial proteins. Clinical features are highly variable but often include myopathy, encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, and stroke-like episodes (MELAS). Diagnosis involves serum lactate, pyruvate, amino acid profile, urine organic acids, muscle biopsy with respiratory chain enzyme analysis, and mitochondrial genome sequencing. Treatment is primarily supportive. Certain vitamins and cofactors (CoQ10, riboflavin, thiamine) may be beneficial in specific subtypes. Valproate is contraindicated in mitochondrial disease due to risk of hepatic toxicity.

title: NIH Guidelines — Connective Tissue Disorders
text: Rare connective tissue disorders include Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutations), Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (multiple gene subtypes), osteogenesis imperfecta (COL1A1/COL1A2), and pseudoxanthoma elasticum (ABCC6). Marfan syndrome requires ophthalmologic, cardiac (aortic root surveillance), and skeletal monitoring. Beta-blockers and ARBs (losartan) are used to slow aortic root dilation. Ehlers-Danlos syndrome management focuses on pain control, joint protection, and physiotherapy. Bisphosphonates reduce fracture risk in osteogenesis imperfecta.

title: CDC Public Health Approach to Rare Diseases
text: The CDC's National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities (NCBDDD) oversees public health programs for several rare diseases including hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell disease, thalassemia), bleeding disorders (hemophilia), and Duchenne muscular dystrophy. CDC's newborn screening quality assurance program supports the detection of over 30 rare conditions at birth. CDC resources include the Hemoglobin Disorders Program, Fragile X Syndrome data and statistics, and surveillance systems for muscular dystrophy.

title: CDC Newborn Screening — Metabolic and Genetic Conditions
text: CDC supports newborn screening (NBS) as the most effective public health intervention for early identification of rare metabolic, endocrine, and genetic conditions. The Recommended Uniform Screening Panel (RUSP) in the US includes conditions such as PKU (phenylketonuria), MSUD (maple syrup urine disease), galactosemia, congenital hypothyroidism, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, SCID, and spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). Early identification allows treatment to begin before symptoms develop, dramatically improving outcomes.

title: CDC Sickle Cell Disease Management Guidelines
text: Sickle cell disease (SCD) is the most common genetic blood disorder worldwide. CDC guidelines emphasize: (1) penicillin prophylaxis from 2 months of age for infection prevention, (2) hydroxyurea therapy to reduce vaso-occlusive crises, (3) transcranial Doppler screening for stroke risk, (4) pneumococcal, meningococcal, and annual influenza vaccination, (5) newborn screening in all states. Acute pain crises should be managed with IV hydration, analgesics (opioids for severe pain), and oxygen. Acute chest syndrome is a medical emergency requiring prompt treatment.

title: CDC Hemophilia and Bleeding Disorders — Clinical Management
text: Hemophilia A and B are X-linked rare bleeding disorders caused by deficiencies of clotting factor VIII and IX respectively. Treatment involves factor replacement therapy (prophylactic or on-demand). Extended half-life factor products reduce infusion frequency. Non-factor therapies (emicizumab/Hemlibra) are approved for hemophilia A with or without inhibitors. Inhibitor development is a major complication requiring immune tolerance induction. All patients should avoid NSAIDs and aspirin. Intramuscular injections should be avoided. Gene therapy is emerging as a curative option.

title: WHO Nutritional Management in Inherited Metabolic Disorders
text: Dietary management is the cornerstone of treatment for many inherited metabolic disorders (IMDs). For phenylketonuria (PKU): phenylalanine-restricted diet with medical formula. For MSUD: branched-chain amino acid (BCAA)-restricted diet. For galactosemia: galactose-free diet from birth. For homocystinuria: methionine-restricted diet, pyridoxine supplementation. For organic acidemias (MMA, PA): protein-restricted diet with essential amino acid supplements. Regular monitoring of metabolites and nutritional status is essential. Dietitian involvement is mandatory in all IMD management plans.

title: NIH Neurological Rare Diseases — Diagnosis and Care
text: The NINDS (National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke) oversees research and clinical guidance for rare neurological diseases. This includes: Huntington's disease (autosomal dominant CAG repeat expansion in HTT gene), Friedreich's ataxia (GAA repeat in FXN gene), spinal muscular atrophy (SMN1 deletions), Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (PMP22 duplications), and neuromuscular junction disorders like myasthenia gravis. Treatment for SMA has been revolutionized by nusinersen (Spinraza), onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma), and risdiplam (Evrysdi). Early treatment in pre-symptomatic infants offers the best outcomes.

title: Diagnostic Delay in Rare Diseases — Clinical Evidence
text: Multiple peer-reviewed studies published in journals indexed by PubMed document that the average diagnostic delay for rare disease patients is 4–8 years from symptom onset. Patients see an average of 7 physicians before receiving a correct diagnosis. Symptoms are frequently misattributed to common conditions. Key factors contributing to diagnostic delay include: low disease prevalence, clinical heterogeneity, lack of specialist awareness, and limited access to genomic testing. Artificial intelligence-assisted phenotyping tools and patient-reported symptom databases are emerging strategies to reduce diagnostic odyssey.

title: WHO Palliative Care for Rare Diseases
text: WHO recognizes that palliative care is an essential component of comprehensive care for patients with progressive or life-limiting rare diseases. Palliative care aims to improve quality of life through prevention and relief of suffering, early identification and treatment of pain, psychological and spiritual support, and assistance to families. For pediatric rare disease patients with no curative options, WHO recommends early integration of palliative care from the time of diagnosis, not only at end of life. Pain management using the WHO analgesic ladder is applicable.

title: Red Cross — Anaphylaxis First Aid
text: Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that can occur in rare disease patients (e.g., during enzyme replacement therapy infusions). Red Cross first aid: (1) Call emergency services (112 in India) immediately. (2) Administer epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into the outer thigh — do NOT delay for antihistamines. (3) Lay the person flat with legs elevated unless they have breathing difficulty. (4) If unconscious and not breathing, begin CPR. (5) A second dose of epinephrine may be given after 5-15 minutes if no improvement. Epinephrine is the only first-line treatment — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only.

title: Red Cross — Seizure First Aid
text: Seizures are common in many rare neurological and metabolic conditions. Red Cross seizure first aid: (1) Protect the person from injury — clear the area, do not restrain movements. (2) Place something soft under the head. (3) Time the seizure duration. (4) Turn the person onto their side (recovery position) to prevent choking. (5) Do NOT put anything in the mouth. (6) Stay with the person until fully conscious. Call 112 if: seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, person does not regain consciousness, a second seizure follows, or the person is pregnant or diabetic.

title: Red Cross — CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
text: CPR is a life-saving technique when someone is unresponsive and not breathing normally. Red Cross CPR steps: (1) Call 112. (2) Place heel of hand on center of chest. (3) Compress at least 2 inches deep, 100-120 compressions per minute. (4) Allow full chest recoil between compressions. (5) If trained: 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions (30:2 ratio). (6) If untrained: continuous hands-only compressions. (7) Use AED as soon as available. (8) Continue until emergency services arrive or person shows signs of life.

title: WHO Emergency Management — Metabolic Crisis in Inherited Metabolic Disorders
text: Patients with inherited metabolic disorders (IMDs) can develop acute metabolic crises triggered by infection, fasting, surgery, or dietary deviation. Emergency management: (1) Stop all protein intake immediately. (2) Provide high glucose infusion (IV dextrose 10%) to suppress catabolism. (3) Correct electrolyte imbalances. (4) For hyperammonemia: IV sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate; consider hemodialysis if ammonia >500 umol/L. (5) For hypoglycemia in fatty acid oxidation defects: IV glucose, avoid fasting. (6) For lactic acidosis in mitochondrial disease: thiamine IV, avoid metformin. Contact a metabolic specialist urgently.

title: WHO Emergency Care — Recognition and Management of Shock
text: Shock is life-threatening circulatory failure. WHO recognizes: (1) Hypovolemic shock: IV crystalloids 20 mL/kg bolus. (2) Septic shock: IV antibiotics within 1 hour, vasopressors. (3) Cardiogenic shock (in rare cardiomyopathies): dobutamine, minimize fluids. (4) Anaphylactic shock: epinephrine IM 0.5 mg (1:1000), IV fluids. Recognition signs: altered consciousness, rapid weak pulse, low blood pressure, pale cold clammy skin, capillary refill >3 seconds.

title: WHO Emergency Care — Airway Management
text: Airway compromise can occur in rare diseases affecting the head and neck (e.g., MPS Hunter syndrome causing tracheal narrowing, angioedema from C1 inhibitor deficiency). Management: (1) Position patient upright or head-tilt chin-lift if unconscious. (2) Clear airway of secretions. (3) Apply oxygen 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask. (4) For angioedema: IV C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate, icatibant (bradykinin antagonist), or fresh frozen plasma. (5) Intubation may be difficult in MPS patients — have surgical airway (cricothyrotomy) as backup.

title: Red Cross — Hypoglycemia First Aid
text: Hypoglycemia is a common emergency in rare metabolic conditions (MCAD, VLCAD, glycogen storage diseases). First aid: (1) If conscious: give 15g fast-acting sugar — 4 glucose tablets, 150 mL fruit juice, or 3 teaspoons sugar in water. (2) Recheck blood sugar in 15 minutes; repeat if still low. (3) Once improved, give a complex carbohydrate snack. (4) If unconscious: do NOT give anything by mouth — call 112. (5) Glucagon injection if available. (6) IV dextrose 50% is the definitive hospital treatment. Prolonged fasting must be avoided in fatty acid oxidation defects.

title: WHO Mental Health First Aid — Psychiatric Crisis
text: Rare disease patients have significantly elevated rates of depression, anxiety, and suicidality due to chronic illness burden, diagnostic odyssey, and social isolation. WHO mental health first aid (ALGEE): (1) Approach, assess, and assist with any crisis. (2) Listen non-judgementally. (3) Give support and information. (4) Encourage appropriate professional help. (5) Encourage other supports. For acute psychiatric emergencies: ensure safety, do not leave the person alone, call emergency services if risk of self-harm is immediate. iCall India helpline: 9152987821.

title: Red Cross — Choking First Aid
text: Choking is a risk in rare diseases affecting swallowing (dysphagia in MPS, neuromuscular conditions). Red Cross: (1) If they can cough or speak, encourage coughing. (2) If unable to cough or speak: call 112. (3) 5 back blows: lean forward, give 5 firm blows between shoulder blades. (4) 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich): stand behind, fist above navel, pull sharply inward and upward. (5) Alternate back blows and abdominal thrusts. (6) If unconscious: begin CPR. For infants: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts (NOT abdominal).

title: WHO Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) Guidelines
text: Dehydration can precipitate acute crises in rare metabolic diseases. WHO ORT for mild-moderate dehydration: WHO ORS — sodium 75 mmol/L, glucose 75 mmol/L; give 75 mL/kg over 4 hours. Signs of severe dehydration requiring IV fluids: sunken eyes, skin turgor >2 seconds, inability to drink, altered consciousness. In metabolic disease patients: consult metabolic team before any glucose or protein-containing IV fluids. IV Ringer's lactate may worsen lactic acidosis in mitochondrial disease — use normal saline.

title: National Policy for Rare Diseases (NPRD) 2021 — India
text: India's National Policy for Rare Diseases 2021 (NPRD 2021) was approved by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. It categorizes rare diseases into three groups: Group 1 — disorders amenable to one-time curative treatment (e.g., bone marrow transplant); Group 2 — diseases requiring long-term treatment with relatively lower costs; Group 3 — diseases requiring long-term costly treatment. Under NPRD 2021, financial support up to INR 50 lakhs per patient is provided from the PM-JAY (Ayushman Bharat) corpus for Group 1 diseases. Centres of Excellence (CoEs) designated for rare disease treatment include AIIMS New Delhi, PGI Chandigarh, SGPGI Lucknow, NIMHANS, and others.

title: NHM Rare Disease Support — Patient Registration and Crowdfunding
text: Under NPRD 2021, the government has established a portal for patient registration for rare diseases at rarediseases.mohfw.gov.in. Patients can register to access financial support. The government supports voluntary crowd-funding for Group 2 and Group 3 rare diseases where government funds are insufficient. Rashtriya Arogya Nidhi (RAN) provides one-time financial assistance to BPL patients for treatment at government hospitals. State governments are encouraged to set up their own rare disease funds and policies.

source: Government of India — Ministry of Social Justice
title: Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPWD) Act 2016 — Rare Disease Coverage
text: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPWD) Act 2016 expanded the list of recognized disabilities from 7 to 21 categories. For rare disease patients, the relevant additions include: Acid attack victims, Blood disorder (hemophilia, sickle cell disease), Intellectual disability (including rare genetic conditions), Specific learning disabilities, Multiple sclerosis, Muscular dystrophy, Chronic neurological conditions, and Multiple disabilities including deaf-blindness. Persons with benchmark disability (40%+ disability certificate) are entitled to: 4% reservation in government jobs, 5% seats reserved in higher education, priority access to government schemes, disability pension, and social security benefits.

source: Government of India — Ministry of Social Justice
title: RPWD Act 2016 — Disability Certificate Process
text: To obtain a disability certificate in India: (1) Visit a government hospital (district hospital or above) with a medical board. (2) Submit required documents: identity proof (Aadhaar), address proof, medical records, passport-size photographs. (3) Medical board assesses percentage of disability. (4) Certificate issued specifying type and percentage of disability. For rare disease patients: bring detailed medical records including genetic test reports, specialist letters, and functional assessments. Udyam-based Unique Disability ID (UDID) card is issued for national portability of benefits. Online application also possible at swavlambancard.gov.in.

title: Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY — Coverage for Rare Diseases
text: Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY) provides health insurance coverage up to INR 5 lakhs per family per year. Under NPRD 2021, Group 1 rare disease patients (diseases amenable to one-time curative treatment) can access additional financial support up to INR 50 lakhs from the NPRD corpus fund on top of PM-JAY. Eligible families: defined by SECC 2011 database and state-specific lists. For rare disease treatment, PM-JAY empanelled hospitals include government Centres of Excellence. Application via Ayushman Bharat portal (pmjay.gov.in) or through Common Service Centres (CSCs).

title: Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi Pariyojana (PMBJP) — Generic Medicine Access
text: The Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi Pariyojana (PMBJP) provides quality generic medicines at significantly lower prices through Jan Aushadhi Kendras (pharmacies). While rare disease-specific enzyme replacement therapies are generally not available here, many supportive and symptomatic medications are available at 50-90% lower cost than branded equivalents. Over 9,000 Jan Aushadhi Kendras are operational across India. Website: janaushadhi.gov.in. Medicines include analgesics, anti-infectives, nutritional supplements, vitamins, and general supportive care drugs commonly used as adjuncts in rare disease management.

title: NHM Newborn Screening — RBSK Programme
text: The Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) under NHM provides newborn screening for congenital conditions including: congenital hypothyroidism, congenital deafness, congenital cataracts, congenital heart disease, and developmental delays. RBSK deploys mobile health teams to screen children from birth to 18 years at government schools, anganwadis, and health facilities. Early detection through RBSK allows referral to District Early Intervention Centres (DEICs). While comprehensive metabolic newborn screening (expanded NBS) is limited in India, pilot programs in states like Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and Maharashtra screen for conditions like PKU and congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

title: ADIP Scheme — Assistive Devices for Persons with Disabilities
text: The Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances (ADIP) scheme provides assistive devices to disabled persons at concessional rates. Eligible devices: wheelchairs, hearing aids, tricycles, crutches, Braille kits, DAISY players, walking sticks, and prosthetics. Eligibility: persons with disability as defined by RPWD Act 2016, with monthly income below INR 20,000 (individual) or INR 22,500 (family). Application through District Collector or state disability department. Camp-based distribution through NGOs. For rare disease patients with mobility or sensory disability, ADIP can provide critical assistive technology free or at highly subsidized rates.

source: Government of India — Ministry of Finance
title: Disability Pension — State and Central Government Schemes
text: India offers disability pension through the National Social Assistance Programme (NSAP): Indira Gandhi National Disability Pension Scheme (IGNDPS) provides INR 300-500/month to persons with severe or multiple disabilities aged 18-79 years from BPL families. State governments supplement this with additional amounts (e.g., Tamil Nadu provides INR 1,500/month total). Application through Block Development Office or Panchayat. In addition, many states have direct benefit transfer (DBT) schemes for disabled individuals. AIIMS and government CoEs may provide subsidized or free treatment for rare disease patients under their own trust funds.

source: Government of India — Ministry of Health
title: CGHS Coverage for Rare Disease Treatment — Government Employees
text: The Central Government Health Scheme (CGHS) covers central government employees and pensioners. CGHS now includes provisions for rare disease treatment in government-designated centres of excellence. Reimbursement for enzyme replacement therapies and other orphan drugs used in rare disease treatment may be processed through CGHS on a case-by-case basis with prior approval. Beneficiaries must submit application with treating specialist's prescription, diagnosis confirmation from a CoE, and cost estimate. ECHS (Ex-Servicemen Contributory Health Scheme) provides similar coverage for retired defence personnel.

title: NHM — NGO and Civil Society Partnership for Rare Diseases
text: NHM actively partners with NGOs and patient advocacy organizations for rare disease support. Key organizations working in rare disease space in India: ORDI (Organization for Rare Diseases India), Indian Council of Medical Genetics (ICMG), Lysosomal Storage Disorders Support Society (LSDSS), Thalassaemia International Federation India chapter, and Rett Syndrome Society of India. These organizations help with: patient registration, access to therapies, expert second opinion, peer support groups, and legal aid for disability rights. The government funds NGOs through NHM for outreach activities in underserved areas.

source: Government of India — Ministry of Health
title: India Emergency Services — Contact Numbers
text: India emergency contact numbers: (1) National Emergency Number: 112 (police, fire, ambulance combined). (2) Ambulance: 108 (most states) or 102 (NHM ambulance for pregnant women, neonates, and seriously ill patients). (3) Disaster Management: 1078. (4) Mental Health Helpline: iCall 9152987821, Vandrevala Foundation 1860-2662-345 (24/7). (5) AIIMS emergency department New Delhi: 011-26588500. (6) PGI Chandigarh emergency: 0172-2756565. For rare disease acute crises: always inform treating rare disease specialist before visiting local emergency room — carry a medical alert card with diagnosis, current medications, and emergency protocols specific to your condition.

title: OMIM #230800 — Gaucher Disease Type 1
text: Gaucher disease type 1 (OMIM #230800) is the most common lysosomal storage disorder, caused by mutations in the GBA gene encoding glucocerebrosidase. Inheritance is autosomal recessive. Prevalence is approximately 1:40,000 in the general population and 1:800 in the Ashkenazi Jewish population. Clinical features include hepatosplenomegaly, bone pain and fractures, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and fatigue. The N370S mutation (most common in Ashkenazi Jews) is associated with non-neuronopathic type 1. Diagnosis: reduced glucocerebrosidase enzyme activity in leukocytes; confirmed by GBA gene sequencing. Biomarker: elevated lyso-Gb1 and chitotriosidase. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (imiglucerase, velaglucerase alfa, taliglucerase alfa) or substrate reduction therapy (miglustat, eliglustat).

title: OMIM #301500 — Fabry Disease
text: Fabry disease (OMIM #301500) is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in the GLA gene encoding alpha-galactosidase A. Hemizygous males are severely affected; heterozygous females have variable penetrance. Prevalence: approximately 1:40,000-60,000 in males. Clinical features: neuropathic pain (acroparesthesia), angiokeratoma, cornea verticillata, hypohidrosis, renal failure, cardiomyopathy (LVH), and stroke. Diagnosis: reduced alpha-galactosidase A enzyme activity in leukocytes (males); GLA gene sequencing (females and for confirmation). Biomarker: elevated plasma and urine lyso-Gb3. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (agalsidase alfa, agalsidase beta), migalastat (pharmacological chaperone for amenable mutations), plus cardiac, renal, and neurological supportive care.

title: OMIM #232300 — Pompe Disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type II)
text: Pompe disease (OMIM #232300) is caused by mutations in the GAA gene encoding acid alpha-glucosidase. Autosomal recessive. Prevalence: approximately 1:40,000. Classic infantile form: hypotonia, cardiomegaly, respiratory failure within first year. Late-onset form: progressive proximal myopathy, respiratory insufficiency without cardiomyopathy. Diagnosis: dried blood spot (DBS) GAA enzyme activity, confirmed by GAA gene sequencing. Included in newborn screening panels in many countries. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy with alglucosidase alfa (Myozyme/Lumizyme) or cipaglucosidase alfa + miglustat (next-generation). Respiratory monitoring and ventilatory support are critical. Patients should avoid prolonged fasting.

title: OMIM #607014 — Mucopolysaccharidosis Type I (Hurler/Scheie Syndrome)
text: Mucopolysaccharidosis type I (MPS I, OMIM #607014) is caused by mutations in the IDUA gene encoding alpha-L-iduronidase. Autosomal recessive. Three clinical subtypes: Hurler (severe), Hurler-Scheie (intermediate), Scheie (attenuated). Clinical features: coarse facies, corneal clouding, hepatosplenomegaly, dysostosis multiplex (skeletal dysplasia), cardiac valve disease, obstructive airway disease, carpal tunnel syndrome, and neurological regression (Hurler). Diagnosis: elevated urinary dermatan sulfate and heparan sulfate; reduced IDUA enzyme activity; IDUA gene sequencing. Treatment: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is preferred for Hurler if done before age 2.5; enzyme replacement therapy (laronidase) for all subtypes. Airway management is challenging — tracheal intubation may be difficult.

title: OMIM #277900 — Wilson Disease
text: Wilson disease (OMIM #277900) is caused by mutations in the ATP7B gene encoding a copper-transporting ATPase. Autosomal recessive. Prevalence: 1:30,000. Onset typically age 5-35 years. Clinical features: hepatic (hepatitis, cirrhosis, acute liver failure), neurological (tremor, dysarthria, dysphagia, psychiatric symptoms), and Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper deposits in cornea visible on slit-lamp). Diagnosis: serum ceruloplasmin (low), 24-hour urine copper (elevated), liver biopsy copper content, ATP7B gene sequencing. Treatment: D-penicillamine or trientine (copper chelation) or zinc acetate (reduces absorption). Dietary copper restriction. Liver transplantation for acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis. Lifelong treatment is required — abrupt discontinuation can cause acute liver failure.

title: OMIM #154700 — Marfan Syndrome
text: Marfan syndrome (OMIM #154700) is caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1. Autosomal dominant. Prevalence: 1:5,000-10,000. Clinical features include tall stature with disproportionately long limbs and fingers (arachnodactyly), scoliosis, pectus deformities, ectopia lentis (lens dislocation), and aortic root dilation (risk of dissection). Diagnosis: Ghent criteria (clinical + genetic). Treatment: aortic root monitoring with echocardiography (every 6-12 months), beta-blockers (atenolol) and/or ARBs (losartan) to slow aortic dilation, surgical aortic root replacement when root diameter >4.5-5 cm. Avoid contact sports and extreme physical exertion. Dura ectasia in lumbar spine is a common complication.

title: OMIM #261600 — Phenylketonuria (PKU)
text: Phenylketonuria (PKU, OMIM #261600) is caused by mutations in the PAH gene encoding phenylalanine hydroxylase. Autosomal recessive. Prevalence: approximately 1:10,000-15,000. Without treatment, elevated phenylalanine causes irreversible intellectual disability, microcephaly, and behavioral problems. Diagnosis by newborn screening (Guthrie test or tandem MS). Treatment: lifelong phenylalanine-restricted diet with medical formula supplementation. Sapropterin (BH4 cofactor) is effective for BH4-responsive PKU (~20-50% of patients). Pegvaliase (PEGylated PAH enzyme) is approved for adults. Blood phenylalanine levels should be maintained at 120-360 umol/L. Maternal PKU (pregnancy in affected women) requires very strict phenylalanine control to prevent fetal harm.

title: OMIM #143100 — Huntington Disease
text: Huntington disease (OMIM #143100) is caused by a CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene (>36 repeats). Autosomal dominant. Prevalence: 5-10 per 100,000. Age of onset typically 30-50 years (juvenile onset if >60 repeats). Clinical features: involuntary choreiform movements, cognitive decline (dementia), and psychiatric symptoms (depression, irritability, obsessive-compulsive behavior). Diagnosis: genetic testing for CAG repeat number. Treatment: symptomatic only — tetrabenazine or deutetrabenazine for chorea; antidepressants, antipsychotics for psychiatric symptoms; physiotherapy, speech therapy, nutritional support. Genetic counselling is essential for at-risk family members. Predictive testing involves psychological preparation and counselling.

title: OMIM #105400 — Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)
text: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS, OMIM #105400) involves degeneration of upper and lower motor neurons. Most cases are sporadic; ~10% are familial (SOD1, C9orf72, FUS, TARDBP mutations). Prevalence: 4-6 per 100,000. Median survival 2-5 years from symptom onset. Clinical features: progressive muscle weakness and atrophy, dysphagia, dysarthria, dyspnea, fasciculations. Diagnosis: El Escorial criteria (clinical + EMG + MRI to exclude other causes). Treatment: riluzole (modestly slows progression) and edaravone (for selected patients with early-stage disease). Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) improves quality of life and extends survival. Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) for dysphagia and nutritional support. Multidisciplinary ALS clinic is the gold standard of care.

title: OMIM #253300 — Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
text: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA, OMIM #253300) is caused by deletions or mutations in the SMN1 gene. Autosomal recessive. Prevalence: 1:10,000. Types: SMA Type 1 (most severe, onset <6 months, never sits), Type 2 (onset 6-18 months, never walks), Type 3 (onset >18 months, ambulant), Type 4 (adult onset). SMN2 copy number modifies disease severity. Treatment has been revolutionized: nusinersen (intrathecal antisense oligonucleotide), onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi (gene therapy, approved for <2 years or <21 kg), risdiplam (oral SMN2 splicing modifier). Pre-symptomatic treatment (via newborn screening) results in dramatically better outcomes. Respiratory support and nutritional management are critical.

title: OMIM #219700 — Cystic Fibrosis
text: Cystic fibrosis (CF, OMIM #219700) is caused by mutations in the CFTR gene. Autosomal recessive. Prevalence: approximately 1:2,500-3,500 in Caucasians (lower in Asian populations). Clinical features: recurrent pulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, meconium ileus, male infertility (bilateral absence of vas deferens), and CF-related diabetes. Most common mutation: F508del (>70% of CF alleles). Diagnosis: sweat chloride test >60 mmol/L, confirmed by CFTR gene sequencing. Treatment: CFTR modulator therapy (ivacaftor, lumacaftor-ivacaftor, tezacaftor-ivacaftor, elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor/Trikafta) has transformed outcomes for eligible genotypes. Standard care: airway clearance therapy, antibiotics, pancreatic enzyme replacement, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.

title: OMIM #310200 — Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD)
text: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD, OMIM #310200) is caused by mutations in the DMD gene on chromosome X. X-linked recessive. Prevalence: 1:3,500-5,000 male births. Clinical features: progressive muscle weakness beginning by age 5, loss of ambulation by age 12, cardiomyopathy, and respiratory failure. Diagnosis: elevated serum CK, muscle biopsy (absent dystrophin staining), DMD gene deletion/duplication analysis, sequencing. Treatment: corticosteroids (deflazacort or prednisolone) remain the cornerstone — delay loss of ambulation by 2-3 years. Exon-skipping therapies (eteplirsen for exon 51, golodirsen for exon 53, viltolarsen for exon 53) restore partial dystrophin. Cardiac monitoring (ACE inhibitors/ARBs for cardiomyopathy) and respiratory support (NIV) are essential. Multidisciplinary care at a neuromuscular centre.

title: Orphanet — Rare Disease Definition and Classification
text: Orphanet is the reference portal for information on rare diseases and orphan drugs, maintained by INSERM (France) and partners worldwide. Orphanet assigns ORPHA codes (unique identifiers) to all rare diseases. As of 2024, Orphanet catalogues over 6,000 rare diseases. Rare diseases are classified by: clinical group (e.g., metabolic, neuromuscular, dermatological), gene ontology, HPO (Human Phenotype Ontology) terms, and inheritance pattern. Orphanet provides: disease summaries, expert centre directories, clinical trials listings, support group contacts, and prevalence data. Orphanet is integrated into clinical databases, EHR systems, and diagnostic tools worldwide.

title: Orphanet — Diagnostic Criteria and HPO Phenotyping
text: Orphanet uses Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO) terms to standardize the description of rare disease phenotypes. HPO terms are structured medical descriptors (e.g., HP:0001250 for seizures, HP:0002240 for hepatomegaly, HP:0001332 for dystonia). Each Orphanet disease entry lists: mandatory symptoms (frequency: very frequent >80%), frequent symptoms (30-79%), occasional symptoms (5-29%), and rare symptoms (<5%). Phenotype-driven diagnosis: when a patient's HPO terms are matched against Orphanet disease phenotypes, candidate diagnoses are ranked by phenotypic similarity score. Tools using this: PhenoTips, Phenomizer, LIRICAL, Phenix.

title: Orphanet — Inheritance Patterns in Rare Diseases
text: Orphanet classifies rare disease inheritance into: (1) Autosomal dominant (AD): one mutated allele sufficient; 50% recurrence risk per child; de novo mutations common. (2) Autosomal recessive (AR): two mutated alleles required; 25% recurrence risk per child if both parents are carriers. (3) X-linked recessive (XLR): affects hemizygous males primarily; females are carriers. (4) X-linked dominant (XLD): affects males and females; often lethal in males. (5) Mitochondrial: maternally inherited (mtDNA mutations); nuclear gene mutations follow standard Mendelian inheritance. (6) Multifactorial. (7) Somatic (mosaic). Knowing the inheritance pattern is critical for genetic counselling and family planning decisions.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:355 — Gaucher Disease
text: Orphanet ORPHA:355 — Gaucher Disease. Gaucher disease is the most frequent lysosomal storage disease, caused by glucocerebrosidase deficiency (GBA gene). Three subtypes: Type 1 (non-neuronopathic, most common), Type 2 (acute neuronopathic, severe, fatal in infancy), Type 3 (chronic neuronopathic). HPO phenotype (Type 1): hepatomegaly (HP:0002240), splenomegaly (HP:0001744), bone pain (HP:0002653), anemia (HP:0001903), thrombocytopenia (HP:0001873), Erlenmeyer flask deformity of femur. Carrier frequency: 1:400 in general population, much higher in Ashkenazi Jews. Disease prevalence: 1:40,000. Autosomal recessive. Expert centres in India: AIIMS New Delhi (Department of Biochemistry and Pediatrics), Nair Hospital Mumbai.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:79361 — Epidermolysis Bullosa
text: Orphanet ORPHA:79361 — Epidermolysis Bullosa (EB). EB is a group of rare inherited skin disorders characterized by extreme skin fragility and blistering with minimal trauma. Major subtypes: EB simplex (EBS, keratin mutations), junctional EB (JEB, laminin/integrin mutations), dystrophic EB (DEB, COL7A1 mutations), Kindler syndrome. HPO phenotype: skin blistering (HP:0008066), milia (HP:0001056), nail dystrophy (HP:0001甲), esophageal stenosis (HP:0002565), anemia (HP:0001903). Dystrophic EB: squamous cell carcinoma is a life-threatening complication. Treatment: wound care with non-adhesive silicone dressings, nutritional support, pain management, surveillance for malignancy. Gene therapy (Vyjuvek/beremagene geperpavec) is approved for DEB caused by COL7A1 mutations. DEBRA India is the patient advocacy organization.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:904 — Williams Syndrome
text: Orphanet ORPHA:904 — Williams-Beuren Syndrome (Williams Syndrome). Caused by a microdeletion at chromosome 7q11.23 encompassing the ELN gene (elastin). Prevalence: 1:7,500-20,000. Not typically inherited (de novo in most cases); 50% recurrence risk if parent is affected. Clinical features: supravalvular aortic stenosis (SVAS), peripheral pulmonary stenosis, characteristic elfin facies, intellectual disability (mild-moderate), hypersensitivity to sound, strong social personality, hypercalcemia in infancy. Diagnosis: FISH or chromosomal microarray (CMA) showing 7q11.23 deletion. Management: cardiac follow-up for SVAS, calcium restriction in infancy if hypercalcemic, developmental support, speech therapy, special education, social skills support.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:778 — Rett Syndrome
text: Orphanet ORPHA:778 — Rett Syndrome. Caused by mutations in the MECP2 gene on the X chromosome. X-linked dominant; predominantly affects females (males usually die in utero or neonatally). Prevalence: 1:10,000-15,000 females. Clinical progression: (1) Early onset stagnation (6-18 months), (2) Rapid regression — loss of purposeful hand use, language; hand stereotypies, (3) Pseudo-stationary period (stabilization with persisting deficits), (4) Late motor deterioration. Features: breathing irregularities, scoliosis, seizures, autonomic dysfunction, GI dysmotility. Trofinetide (Daybue) is FDA-approved for Rett syndrome. Supportive care: anticonvulsants, physiotherapy, nutritional support. Rett Syndrome Society of India provides patient advocacy.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:803 — Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) Overview
text: Orphanet ORPHA:803 — Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). ALS is classified as a rare disease (prevalence 4-8 per 100,000). Orphanet categorizes ALS subtypes by causative gene: SOD1-ALS (ORPHA:228119), C9orf72-ALS (ORPHA:395561), FUS-ALS (ORPHA:317349), TARDBP-ALS. The HPO phenotype includes: upper motor neuron signs (HP:0002493), lower motor neuron signs (HP:0007269), amyotrophy (HP:0003202), bulbar palsy (HP:0001283), respiratory insufficiency. ALS patient registry: ENCALS (European) and ALSA (ALS Association, USA). In India, ALS support is provided by ALS India Foundation. Multidisciplinary clinics are available at NIMHANS Bangalore and AIIMS New Delhi.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:848 — Beta-Thalassemia
text: Orphanet ORPHA:848 — Beta-Thalassemia. Beta-thalassemia is caused by mutations in the HBB gene reducing or abolishing beta-globin chain synthesis. Autosomal recessive. India has one of the highest carrier rates globally — estimated 3-4% of the population are carriers. Clinical forms: beta-thalassemia major (transfusion-dependent), intermedia (variable), and minor (carrier/trait). Major: severe anemia from 6 months, massive hepatosplenomegaly, frontal bossing, growth retardation. Treatment: regular packed RBC transfusions + chelation therapy (deferasirox, deferoxamine, deferiprone) for transfusion-dependent patients. Curative: allogeneic HSCT. Gene therapy (betibeglogene/Zynteglo) is approved in USA/EU. Thalassaemia India and Thalassaemia International Federation India chapter are active patient organizations.

title: Orphanet ORPHA:232 — Sickle Cell Disease
text: Orphanet ORPHA:232 — Sickle Cell Disease (SCD). Caused by a specific mutation in HBB gene (Glu6Val, rs334). Autosomal recessive. Highly prevalent in India, particularly in tribal populations of Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Chattisgarh, Gujarat, and Odisha. Clinical features: vaso-occlusive pain crises, acute chest syndrome, splenic sequestration, stroke, avascular necrosis, priapism, chronic hemolysis. Newborn screening is being implemented in high-prevalence Indian states. Treatment: hydroxyurea (reduces crises frequency), chronic transfusion program for high-stroke-risk patients, penicillin prophylaxis, comprehensive vaccination, voxelotor, crizanlizumab. Curative: HSCT or gene therapy (exagamglogene autotemcel/Casgevy - CRISPR-based, FDA approved 2023). National Sickle Cell Anaemia Elimination Mission (NSCAEM) is the Indian government's targeted program.
